name: notation_drift
description: Proof uses the same symbol with two different meanings without a renaming declaration. check-notation-consistency should flag multiple_meanings as a critical_error.
statement: Let $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ be continuous. Then $f$ attains its maximum on $[0, 1]$.
proof:
## Notation

- $f$ — a continuous real-valued function on $\mathbb{R}$, source: self, statement.
- $x_0$ — the maximizing point on $[0, 1]$, source: self, Definition below.

## Assumptions

- H1: $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous.

# theorem main

## statement

Let $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ be continuous. Then $f$ attains its maximum on $[0, 1]$.

## proof

Since $[0, 1]$ is compact and $f$ is continuous, the image $f([0, 1])$ is compact. [cite: Rudin PMA, Thm 4.14]

Let $f$ denote the supremum of $f([0, 1])$; this is finite. [def: supremum]

There exists $x_0 \in [0, 1]$ with $f(x_0) = f$. [from L.1]
